explanation_md: `p` points to an `A` object. `dynamic_cast<A*>(p)` succeeds (returns a valid pointer), so `pa->hello()` prints `A`. `dynamic_cast<B*>(p)` fails because `*p` is an `A`, not a `B` — it returns `nullptr`. The `else` branch prints `not B`.

prompt_md: What does `dynamic_cast<Derived*>(base_ptr)` return when the object pointed to by `base_ptr` is NOT a `Derived` or a class derived from `Derived`?

options.a: A pointer to the `Base` sub-object of the nearest compatible type.
options.c: An uninitialized pointer — behavior is undefined.

explanation_md: For pointer casts, `dynamic_cast` returns `nullptr` on failure. Throwing `std::bad_cast` is the behavior for reference casts (e.g. `dynamic_cast<Derived&>(base_ref)`), not pointer casts. Always check the returned pointer for `nullptr` before using it.

prompt_md: Why can `dynamic_cast` only be used with polymorphic classes?

options.a: Non-polymorphic classes do not support inheritance.
options.b: The cast relies on RTTI stored alongside the vtable, which only exists for classes with at least one virtual function.
options.c: Non-polymorphic class pointers are incompatible with the `dynamic_cast` operator syntax.
options.d: The linker strips type information for non-polymorphic classes to reduce binary size.

explanation_md: `dynamic_cast` performs a runtime type check using RTTI (Run-Time Type Information). RTTI is only generated for polymorphic classes — those with at least one virtual function — because it is stored alongside the vtable. Applying `dynamic_cast` to a non-polymorphic class is a compile error.

prompt_md: A function receives an `Animal*` and calls `dynamic_cast<Dog*>`, `dynamic_cast<Cat*>`, and `dynamic_cast<Bird*>` in a chain. A senior developer flags this code. What is their most likely concern?

options.a: The function performs three runtime casts, each of which may throw an exception.
options.b: The behavior differs per derived type, suggesting a virtual function in the base class would be more appropriate.
options.c: `dynamic_cast` cannot be used more than once on the same pointer.
options.d: The casts run in the wrong order and will always return `nullptr`.

explanation_md: A long `dynamic_cast` chain that dispatches differently per derived type is a code smell. If each derived type has distinct behavior for the same conceptual operation, that operation belongs in a virtual function in the base class. `dynamic_cast` is appropriate when you need derived-only functionality that genuinely cannot be expressed through the base interface.
